text.title: Campaign Structure and Objective Selection
text.summary: Three-tier Campaign → Ad Set → Ad model, objective-to-goal mapping, and CBO vs ad-set budget decision.

section.title: Three-Tier Structure
section.p: Campaign: defines objective. Ad Set: defines audience, placements, schedule, budget. Ad: defines creative and copy.

rule.statement: Never mix objectives within a campaign — each campaign optimizes for exactly one objective signal.
rule.rationale: Meta's delivery system is objective-aware at the campaign level; splitting objectives forces the algorithm to optimize for incompatible signals simultaneously.

list.item: Awareness → reach and impressions (brand, new markets)
Traffic → link clicks or landing page views (content promotion)
Leads → Lead Instant Forms or website lead events
Sales → Purchase pixel event or catalog sales

rule.statement: For revenue-driven campaigns, always choose Sales or Leads objective — never Traffic, even if conversion volume is low initially.
rule.rationale: Traffic objective optimizes for the cheapest click, not the most likely buyer; this fills the funnel with high-bounce, low-intent visits that look good in CPCs but produce zero conversions.

list.item: CBO (Campaign Budget Optimization) — Meta distributes across ad sets dynamically; best when testing multiple audiences with equal starting budgets
Ad-Set Budget — fixed per ad set; use when one ad set must never be starved by others (e.g. retargeting)

rule.statement: Start with CBO at $20-50/day minimum and let Meta distribute; switch to ad-set budget only if a specific ad set is being chronically under-funded by CBO after 7+ days.
rule.rationale: CBO with sufficient total budget finds efficient delivery faster than manual allocation; premature manual allocation forces sub-optimal fixed splits.
